coredata.dc:title: Waste-derived carbon nanostructures (WD-CNs): An innovative step toward waste to treasury
coredata.dc:creator: Omar, Rishabh Anand
Talreja, Neetu
Chuhan, Divya
Ashfaq, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: With the growing population, the accumulation of waste materials (WMs) (industrial/household waste) in the environment incessantly increases, affecting human health. Additionally, it affects the climate and ecosystem of terrestrial and water habitats, thereby needing effective management technology to control environmental pollution. In this aspect, managing these WMs to develop products that mitigate the associated issues is necessary. Researchers continue to focus on WMs management by adopting a circular economy. These WMs convert into useful/value-added products such as polymers and nanomaterials (NMs), especially carbon nanomaterials (CNs). The conversion/transformation of waste material into useful products is one of the best solutions for managing waste. Waste-derived CNs (WD-CNs) have established boundless promises for numerous applications like environmental remediation, energy, catalysts, sensors, and biomedical applications. This review paper discusses the several sources of waste material (agricultural, plastic, industrial, biomass, and other) transforming into WD-CNs, such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs), biochar, graphene, carbon nanofibers (CNFs), carbon dots, etc., are extensively elaborated and their application. The impact of metal doping within the WD-CNs is briefly discussed, along with their applicability to end applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the growing population, the accumulation of waste materials (WMs) (industrial/household waste) in the environment incessantly increases, affecting human health. Additionally, it affects the climate and ecosystem of terrestrial and water habitats, thereby needing effective management technology to control environmental pollution. In this aspect, managing these WMs to develop products that mitigate the associated issues is necessary. Researchers continue to focus on WMs management by adopting a circular economy. These WMs convert into useful/value-added products such as polymers and nanomaterials (NMs), especially carbon nanomaterials (CNs). The conversion/transformation of waste material into useful products is one of the best solutions for managing waste. Waste-derived CNs (WD-CNs) have established boundless promises for numerous applications like environmental remediation, energy, catalysts, sensors, and biomedical applications. This review paper discusses the several sources of waste material (agricultural, plastic, industrial, biomass, and other) transforming into WD-CNs, such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs), biochar, graphene, carbon nanofibers (CNFs), carbon dots, etc., are extensively elaborated and their application. The impact of metal doping within the WD-CNs is briefly discussed, along with their applicability to end applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The incessantly increasing global population has led to rapid and exponential growth in development due to growing demands day after day, which significantly increased the production of different kinds of waste. Approximately 8 billion tons of waste are produced yearly, causing severe environmental and health issues and creating a massive challenge for society and the scientific community. Plastic waste (PW) has increased significantly as numerous industries, including transportation, automobiles, electronics, energy storage devices, food packaging, and everywhere, have been used extensively to some extent. Moreover, agricultural waste also produces many toxic products/gases on burning, usually released gases (including nitrogen oxide, carbon monoxide, and sulfur dioxide) into the atmosphere. The situation becomes more complicated due to waste production in every aspect, including plastics, agricultural, organic, and electronics waste (Balat, 2007; Cook and Beyea, 2000; Tatrari et al., 2021). According to reports in 2011, plastic production reached around 280 million tons, and approximately 10% weight of plastic is waste per year. Similarly, agricultural waste is ∼146 billion tons, and ∼100 billion tons of household waste is produced annually globally (Berndes, 2002; Malode et al., 2023; Moreira, 2005; Parikka, 2004; Searchinger et al., 2008). Moreover, a recent estimation shows that approximately 2.01 billion of solid waste (SW) is generated per year, and around 33% of waste is unmanaged (Khan et al., 2022a). The growing problem of waste generation poses a multifaceted challenge with far-reaching consequences for the environment, public health, and sustainable development (Mohammadi et al., 2023). Rapid urbanization, population growth, and increased consumption patterns contribute to the escalating volumes of waste, placing a strain on waste management infrastructure. Improper disposal methods, such as landfilling and incineration, lead to soil and water pollution, while the decomposition of organic waste (OW) releases harmful greenhouse gases (Kumar et al., 2022). The accumulation of non-biodegradable materials exacerbates land degradation and disrupts ecosystems (Hemidat et al., 2022). Additionally, the unequal distribution of waste management resources and the lack of awareness about responsible waste practices further compound the issue, underscoring the urgent need for comprehensive and innovative solutions to address the global waste crisis (Khan et al., 2022a).

Managing such massive waste materials is the greatest challenge nowadays. The conversion of waste into useful products holds significant potential to mitigate the environmental impact of waste generation and foster a more sustainable approach to resource management; by transforming waste materials into valuable products, such as recycled goods, biofuels, or compost, we can reduce the burden on landfills and minimize the extraction of raw materials from the environment (Khan et al., 2022b). This process not only conserves natural resources but also helps decrease greenhouse gas emissions associated with traditional waste disposal methods. Moreover, waste-to-product conversion contributes to the circular economy, promoting a closed-loop system where materials are continually reused, thereby lessening the overall environmental footprint. The economic benefits are noteworthy as well, as the creation of new industries centred around waste conversion can generate jobs and stimulate innovation, fostering a more resilient and resource-efficient society (Amin et al., 2022; Ashokkumar et al., 2022; Weir et al., 2022). In this aspect, managing these wastes in developing products that mitigate the associated issues is necessary. Researchers continue to focus on managing WMs by adopting a circular economy, which converts WMs into useful/value-added products such as polymers and nanomaterials (NMs).

Engineered NMs have established boundless promises for numerous applications like environmental remediation, energy, catalyst, biomedical, and sensor applications due to their unique physio-chemical properties. WMs are converted into valuable products by a thermal decomposition process at different temperatures and heating rates, and the process is called pyrolysis. Pyrolysis involves the thermal decomposition of WMs in an air-free environment. Gaseous, liquid, and solid phase products can be obtained from pyrolysis by changing the physical parameters of the process, such as temperature and pressure (Donate, 2014). For example, gasoline is produced from waste plastic and steel by enhancing the temperature in the pyrolysis process (Kongkarat et al., 2013). Biofuels and alcohols were also synthesized from the organic waste (OW) through a high-temperature pyrolysis process (Deng et al., 2016b). In a study, Yunos et al. synthesized a substitute for metallurgical coke in steel marketing using palm shells (agricultural waste) (Yunos et al., 2012). Automotive waste, like glass, was also transformed as a resource for synthesizing silicon nanoparticles and ferrosilicon alloys. Additionally, high-strength ceramics were also prepared using WMs (Farzana et al., 2014). An advanced ceramic known as mullite with thermal shock and creep-resistant properties was synthesized from silica waste by Yugeswaran et al. using the arc plasma method (Yugeswaran et al., 2011). The conversion of WMs into marketable products such as ceramics and steel attracts researchers to synthesize carbon-based nanomaterials (CNs) such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs), carbon nanofibers (CNFs), biochar, graphene, etc., from the WMs (Afreen et al., 2018; Deng et al., 2016b; Dresselhaus and Terrones, 2013; Omar et al., 2020). These CNs materials are further used in various applications such as environmental remediation, sensors, catalysts, and agriculture due to their high porosity, easier of functionalize, high surface area, electrical conductivity, and various physicochemical properties (Ashfaq et al., 2016, 2017a, 2017b; Dresselhaus and Terrones, 2013; Patel et al., 2019; Talreja et al., 2014, 2020).

Numerous synthesis processes, such as pyrolysis, exfoliation, and chemical vapour deposition (CVD), have been used to develop CNs (Manawi et al., 2018; Talreja et al., 2023; Zhou et al., 2021). However, producing these materials from WMs might be environmentally friendly in several prospects. The main challenge in waste conversion is to achieve similar quality and properties of CNs synthesized by the other conventional methods. Also, an environment-friendly production at a large scale with low cost (Zhou et al., 2021). Synthesis of CNs from waste as a starting point will likely reduce the fabrication cost and solid waste volume. Additionally, reusing of WMs is a positive way to recycle technology (Abd Elkodous et al., 2022; Chung et al., 2017; Gu et al., 2019; Jain et al., 2016; Konikkara et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2015). Wastes with a high carbon content, particularly used plastics, are important precursors to synthesizing CNs. Fig. 1 shows the graphical illustration of the WMs to products and its applications.

This review is focused on the synthesis of WD-CNs from WMs, the impact of WD-CNs in a circular economy, and their applications. We also discuss strategies to improve their applicability towards end applications. Finally, we discussed the future aspect of the WD-CNs and their role in developing green environments. We believe that this review article provides newer insight into WD-CNs from different sources that might be beneficial to researchers in designing newer materials from WMs. Additionally, this approach of the WD-CNs might become a next-generation approach toward a circular economy.

The growing population has led to unceasing growth in the development of society that significantly increases the production of WMs. The WMs continuously increase environmental pollution, one severe global threat. In this aspect, utilizing WMs to products might be beneficial to resolve environmental issues and the development of society. The advancement of nanotechnology and material sciences has brought new heights to the scientific community. Incorporating waste management technology with solid waste by developing NMs is one of the greatest steps toward a circular economy. The WD-CNs, such as hydrochar, biochar, CNFs, CNTs, and activated Carbon, have been synthesized by using various processes, including gasification, pyrolysis, torrefaction, hydrothermal carbonization, and isothermal carbonization; Russo and Maugeri, 2021; Yuan et al., 2021). Fig. 2 shows the graphical representation of the WD-CNs from different sources.

Gasification is a complex thermo-chemical process producing syngas products, mainly hydrogen from carbonaceous materials such as petroleum coke, biomass, coal, etc. The process starts at high temperatures and in non-oxygenic conditions (Oliveira et al., 2022; You et al., 2017). Since 2001, as an increment in fuel pricing, the gasification of WMs has received huge attention. The gasification process ends up with the formation of some amount of biochar. However, an ideal gasification process should not produce biochar as the end product (Kambo and Dutta, 2015; Sikarwar et al., 2016). Mechanistically, the biomass gasification process includes four steps, which are drying (at <250 °C), pyrolysis (at 250 to 500 OC), partial combustion, and reduction at 800–1200 °C (Shen et al., 2017). Fig. 3 shows the gasification process setup and development of materials.

Pyrolysis is a cost-effective and environmentally friendly technology for WMs conversion due to the requirement of less energy and emission of less harmful gases compared to other technologies. In general, pyrolysis is used for the thermal decomposition of organic compounds in OW to produce biochar, syngas, and bio-oil (Liu et al., 2017; Yao et al., 2018). The process is performed under an inert atmosphere at a temperature range of 300–900 °C. In the past few years, various pyrolysis processes have been developed. The temperature range, heating rate, and holding time are important in the product's properties. In past studies, the mechanism of the pyrolysis process is well analyzed (Cha et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2014). The major reactions in the pyrolysis pathway include cracking, decarboxylation, hydrocracking, hydrodeoxygenation, and dehydrogenation (Nzihou et al., 2019). Based on operating parameters, the pyrolysis process can be classified into four different categories, which are slow pyrolysis (carried out at 350–900 °C at the slow heating rate (5–7 °C min−1) for long residence time (≤1 h)), fast pyrolysis (performed at 500 °C at the fast heating rate (300–800 °C min−1) and short resident time (≤10 Sec), flash pyrolysis (kind of fast pyrolysis process conducted at 700–1100 °C at a very fast heating rate (≥1100 OC min−1) and very short residence time (≤2 s)) and vacuum pyrolysis (a process of thermal degradation of OW at low pressure (<0.02 MPa) and in an oxygen environment at the temperature range 300–600 °C) (Ahmad et al., 2012; Li et al., 2020b; Liu et al., 2014, 2017; Özbay et al., 2001; Zhao et al., 2018). Fig. 4 shows the schematic representation of the pyrolysis reactor.

Torrefaction is a mild pyrolysis process that includes dry and wet types. The dry torrefaction process was conducted at 200–300 °C in an inert atmosphere for 30–60 min. However, the wet torrefaction process is a hydrothermal carbonization process performed at approximately 200–250 °C for 110–150 min (Acharya et al., 2015; Liu and Balasubramanian, 2014; Waheed et al., 2022). The dry torrefaction process product is suitable for low-moisture WMs. However, the high moisture containing WMs required pre-drying for the involvement in the dry torrefaction process. The torrefaction process converts the OW with higher physicochemical properties. Therefore, it gained more attention in the field of bioenergy (Acharya et al., 2015; Shi et al., 2023; Sun, 2023). Mechanistically, the waste conversion by the torrefaction process involves decarboxylation, dehydration, de-carbonylation, de-methoxylation, intermolecular derangements, condensation, and aromatization chemical reactions (Ivanovski et al., 2023; Yuan et al., 2021). In the dry torrefaction process, the end product is not biochar because pyrolysis is only the start of the process; however, the torrefied products also contain volatile material and show the physicochemical properties between the biochar and organic materials.

The hydrothermal carbonization process can be referred to as the thermochemical conversion of OW in subcritical or hot compressed water at the temperature range of 180–265 °C under a pressured environment (up to 6.0 MPa) for 5–240 min (Cavali et al., 2023b; Czerwińska et al., 2022; Maniscalco et al., 2020). The hydrothermal carbonization process is considered an alternative technique to pyrolysis, gasification, and dry torrefaction because moisture-rich OW can be directly utilized (Wang et al., 2018b; Wu et al., 2023). In this process, hydrolysis of hemicellulose or OW occurs, depolymerizing hemicellulose into monomers and oligomers (Bridgwater, 2012). In the dry waste conversion process, biochar is mostly formed as the end product of the process, which is separated. However, hydrochar is formed in the hydrothermal carbonization process, which needs to be separated from the solution (Kambo and Dutta, 2015; Khalaf et al., 2023). Fig. 5 . Shows the schematic illustrations of the hydrothermal treatment of waste biomass and their applications.

At first, ionic liquids were used as electrolytes for batteries (Zhu et al., 2018). In the later applications, they also used liquid adsorbing agents in the desulfurization process (Omar et al., 2022) due to their good solubility, reduced solvent velocity, and good thermal stability are used in various applications, including waster conversion. The higher solubility of ionic liquids makes them a suitable candidate to use as a beginning or precursor solvent for synthesizing highly porous value-added material from waste. The ionothermal carbonization process is conducted at the temperature range of 160–240 °C in an unsealed vessel reactor, where ionic liquids play the role of catalyst and/or proton-generating agent (Deng et al., 2016a; Lin et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2014). Compared to other techniques, the end products of the ionothermal carbonization process retain more surface area. Additionally, the ionic liquids used for the process can be reused or recycled by some simple steps. However, due to the high cost of ionic liquids, they are still breaking their wide exposure in industrial applications (Cibien et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2012).

WD-CNs can be synthesized by various methods, including gasification, pyrolysis, torrefaction, hydrothermal carbonization, and ionothermal carbonization. Each process has its own impact based on the type of waste, the desired product, and the availability of the resources (Manasa et al., 2022). The choice of the best method depends on the specific waste stream, the desired end products, and the local context. Factors such as energy efficiency, scalability, environmental impact, and the potential for by-product utilization should be considered. Additionally, economic feasibility and regulatory compliance play crucial roles in determining the suitability of a waste-to-carbon conversion method for a particular application or location (Benítez et al., 2022). It's often beneficial to conduct a comprehensive life cycle analysis to evaluate the overall sustainability of a given process.

The synthesis cost of converting WMs to CNs can vary depending on the specific technology or process used, the type of WMs, the conversion process, including temperatures, gasses used for conversion, and various other conditions that vary the conversion cost. Herein, we discussed waste to CNs conversion, and their cost factor discussed as follows: (1) Gasification: Gasification converts plastic waste into synthesis gas (syngas), which is a mixture of carbon monoxide and hydrogen by reacting the plastics with a controlled amount of oxygen and/or steam. Similar to pyrolysis, gasification costs can be influenced by factors such as scale, technology efficiency, and energy costs (Al-asadi et al., 2020; Shah et al., 2023). (2) Pyrolysis: involves heating PW in the absence of oxygen to break it down into smaller hydrocarbons, gases, and carbon-rich char. The cost of pyrolysis can depend on factors such as the scale of the operation, the type of plastics being processed, the efficiency of the equipment, and energy costs for the heating process (Catalan et al., 2023; Xayachak et al., 2022). (3) Carbonization: involves heating PW in the presence of a catalyst to produce carbon and other byproducts. The cost of carbonization can be influenced by the catalyst used, process efficiency, and energy requirements (Djandja et al., 2023), and (4) Hydrothermal Carbonization, Hydrothermal carbonization uses water under high temperature and pressure to convert organic materials, including plastics, into carbon-rich materials. The cost of hydrothermal carbonization can be influenced by the equipment used, energy costs, and process efficiency (Cavali et al., 2023a). It's essential to note that the technology landscape is dynamic, and new methods may emerge over time. Additionally, the economic viability of these processes can be influenced by factors such as local energy prices, regulatory environments, and market demand for the resulting carbon products (Mahapatra et al., 2022). The synthesis cost may include capital costs for equipment, operational costs, and maintenance expenses (Jaria et al., 2022). It's advisable to consider a life cycle analysis to assess the environmental and economic sustainability of such processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: WD-CNs from different types of waste materials
ce:section.ce:para: The waste material is present in the environment in different forms (organic and inorganic), which are converted into CNs by different processes depending on the type of the waste material. Usually, converting WMs into marketable products, mainly ceramics, and steel, attracts researchers to synthesize WD-CNs such as CNTs, CNFs, biochar, graphene, etc. The following section discusses the different sources/types of waste materials and their conversion to CNs.

Usually, Cellulose, hemicellulose, chitosan, animal oil, and vegetable oil extracted from turpentine, coconut, neem, palm, eucalyptus, etc., are considered in OW, which are the common precursor for the synthesis of highly pure CNs (Aydıncak et al., 2012; Chen et al., 2018; Deng et al., 2016b; El Korhani et al., 2013; Huang et al., 2017; Lee et al., 2013a; Li et al., 2020a; Suriani et al., 2013; Tiwari et al., 2022; Zhao et al., 2021). For instance, Zhao et al. synthesized graphene from pomelo peel using different treatment processes. The data indicate that the graphene was successfully synthesized by using a hydrothermal process. The thickness and porosity of the WD-graphene might be easily tuned with the changing ratio of acetic acid-H2O2. Fig. 6 . Shows the schematic representation of the WD-graphene from pomelo peel and SEM images of synthesized WD-graphene with different treatments. The SEM images clearly indicate that the different ratios of acetic acid-H2O2 affect the thickness and porous texture of the graphene (Zhao et al., 2021). Another study focused on palm oil was used to synthesize CNTs with prominent quality due to low H content, which leads to minimizing the amorphous carbon synthesis (Wu et al., 2019). Suraini et al. have used waste cooking palm oil to synthesize vertically aligned CNTs (Suriani et al., 2015). Instead of CNTs, Sekar et al. have used rice husk-based OW to synthesize silicon nanocrystal decorated carbon nanocomposite, which is further used in lithium-ion battery applications. To maintain the effectiveness of the end product and surface modification, they simply calcined the waste material along with the magnesiothermic reduction (Sekar et al., 2019). Similarly, Ding et al. have used waste eggshells to synthesize Au–CaCO3 nanocomposite for the electrochemical detection of 4-nitrophenol. Conversion of discarded or waste eggshells could be an effective waste management and conversion approach. The naturally available eggshells have porous structures, which have already been confirmed by scanning electron microscopy in the literature. Therefore, it can easily absorb Au nanoparticles on its surface, making Au–CaCO3 nanocomposite. The nanocomposite was found to be highly stable and selective for the detection of 4 nitro-phenols (Ding et al., 2020). Many other studies have been done on the conversion of OW to CNs and their various applications. Table 1 describes some of the past studies of the synthesis of WD-CNs from OW.

Plastic plays an essential role in our society's daily life due to its light weight, strength, and low cost. The flexibility and cost-effective production make them suitable for numerous applications like construction, packaging, and electronics. The increasing trend of plastic use and the wastage of plastic is one of the greatest challenges nowadays (Aberilla et al., 2020; Achilias et al., 2007; Andrady and Neal, 2009; de Sousa, 2021; Petersen et al., 1995). Usually, plastic wastes (PW) are discharged/dumped into the environmental bodies and broken into micron-sized particles due to environmental effects. These plastics/microplastics are non-biodegradable and contain many toxic compounds, including heavy metal ions that directly/indirectly affect the environment. Recycling technology might resolve such associated issues with plastic by developing next-generation materials/WD-CNs (Gallo et al., 2018; Li et al., 2017; Thushari and Senevirathna, 2020; Wojnowska-Baryła and Bernat, 2022). Numerous studies have focused on synthesizing WD-CNs from plastics (Bazargan and McKay, 2012; Gong et al., 2013, 2014; Mishra et al., 2012; Nath and Sahajwalla, 2011; Ruan et al., 2011; Yang et al., 2010; Zhou et al., 2017). A study suggested that the degradation of plastic waste can produce light hydrocarbons and aromatics. Plastic waste is converted to graphene, CNTs, and other carbonaceous materials (Bazargan and McKay, 2012). For instance, Gong et al. synthesized graphene flakes using waste polypropylene (PP) as precursor material. The reaction was catalyzed by montmorillonite with some organic modifications to achieve a better yield. As per the literature, a uniform mixture of PP, talcum powder, and montmorillonite was placed in a crucible and heated up to 700 °C for 15 min. The carbonized char was produced in the product, which was further immersed in HF and HNO3 after cooling down to remove the impurities of montmorillonite and talcum (by HF) and oxidize the amorphous Carbon (by HNO3). Graphene was obtained by repeated centrifugation and washing of the product solution (Gong et al., 2014). Similarly, Ruan et al. have used waste polystyrene and other waste materials to synthesize single-layered graphene by a unique method. The waste precursor materials contain waste polystyrene, dog faeces, waste chocolate and cookies, cockroach legs, and grass blades, in which the dog faeces were dehydrated before being used in the reaction. Approximately 10 mg of the sample was placed into a Cu foil-supported crucible and heated up to 1050 °C under low pressure for 15 min in a CVD reactor in an Ar and H2 environment. The graphene was synthesized in the back side of the Cu foil (Ruan et al., 2011). Mishra et al. have produced CNTs from waste polystyrene in a CVD reactor. Approximately 10 g polystyrene was taken in a boat, and 1 g Ni salt was taken as a catalyst into another boat. Both the boats were placed into a quartz CVD reaction and heated up to 600–800 °C using an electrically heated furnace under an H2 and Ar environment. After 400 °C, the polystyrene starts dissolving into carbon form in the hot area of Ni. However, the cold area of Ni serves as the CNTs nucleating site, which precipitates the carbon atoms. The process results in the formation of high-quality multi-walled CNTs (Mishra et al., 2012). Wyss et al. synthesized turbostratic flash graphene from plastic waste using a pyrolysis process. The graphene was produced with exceptional dispersion ability and high yield, approximately 2.84 mg/mL. Moreover, the produced graphene significantly improved the compressive strength of the Portland cement (Wyss et al., 2021). Ning et al. synthesized hydrochar from PVC using hydrothermal carbonization and then used it as a solid fuel. The data suggested that the hydrocarbon has plenty of pores and a high degree of carbon. Moreover, the hydrocarbon produced at 250 °C shows high ignition ability (Ning et al., 2020). Aboul-Enein and Awadallah synthesized MW-CNTs from polypropylene using carbonization and CVD process. The data suggested that the produce MW-CNTs have excellent thermal ability (Aboul-Enein and Awadallah, 2018). Many other similar studies have been done on plastic waste transformation into carbonaceous or useful products. Table 2 briefly describes WD-CNs from plastic waste. The data suggested that numerous PWs, mainly CNTs, CNFs, and graphene, have been used to synthesize the WD-CNs. It is important to mention here we can improve the surface area, conductivity, active functional group, porosity, and stability of WD-CNs by controlling the synthesis process, thereby enhancing applicability towards end application.

Water shortages are caused by perilous compounds in polluted water, which also severely intimidate public health. Water pollution is usually caused by IW or a lack of adequate sanitary facilities. The increase in industrial activity with the human population in the twenty-first century has posed a solemn and long-term danger to water supplies. IW, such as waste engine oil, batteries, tires, and polymers, could also be used to synthesize CNs (Deng et al., 2016b). Numerous studies have been done so far that focus on synthesizing WD-CNs from IW (Dunens et al., 2010; Hu et al., 2014a; Mis-Fernández et al., 2012; Mittal et al., 2013; Moghaddasi et al., 2013; Quan et al., 2010; Suriani et al., 2015; TermehYousefi et al., 2014; Wu et al., 2013; Zhou et al., 2017). For instance, Suraini et al. have synthesized quasi-aligned CNTs from waste engine oil due to the presence of a higher carbon content in engine oil than in other oils. The precursor was heated up to 500–700 °C in a CVD reactor in the presence of ferrocene (17.99 wt%). The oxygen content in the waste engine oil maintains the crystallinity of the CNTs because oxygen-containing compounds provide free radicals, leading to stable sp2 hybridization in CNTs (Suriani et al., 2015). Moghaddasi et al. synthesized nanoparticles from waste tire rubber by ball milling for 5 h. The size of nanoparticles was reduced from <500 nm to <50 nm through ball milling (Moghaddasi et al., 2013). Quan et al. have used printed circuit boards (PCBs) to synthesize CNTs. The PCB board was decomposed at 600 °C to obtain the pyrolysis oil enriched in phenol and other aromatic compounds. The pyrolysis oil was mixed with formaldehyde to convert into resin through the polymerization reaction. Finally, the CNTs were obtained by mixing resin with ferrocene and pyrolysis at 900 OC in an N2 atmosphere (Quan et al., 2010). Similarly, Dunens et al. have synthesized multi-walled CNTs from the red mud waste generated from the Bayer process of alumina fabrication. The waste acts as a catalyst due to higher Fe content (20–60%). The reaction was performed in a CVD reactor at 650 °C, where acetylene was used as a carbon source (Dunens et al., 2010). Yaqubi et al. collected carbon black waste and incorporated it with chitosan to produce carbon-chitosan beads for the removal of pharmaceutical compounds (tetracycline and amoxicillin). The data suggested that the produced carbon-chitosan beads significantly remove pharmaceutical compounds (Yaqubi et al., 2021). Ma et al. synthesized Si–N-doped carbon from Si waste of the photovoltaic industry using simple purification and polydopamine coating for lithium-ion batteries. The data suggested that this strategy for the fabrication of anode provides newer insight for next-generation lithium-ion batteries (Ma et al., 2022). Several other studies have also been done on IW conversion to CNs, some briefly described in Table 3 .

Several other waste materials (excluding the above categories) have also been used to synthesize CNs. Akhawan et al. have used industrial carbonaceous waste to synthesize graphene oxide, reduced graphene oxide, and replaced the economically inefficient highly pyrolytic graphite. The precursor material was wrapped into aluminium foil and heated at 400–500 °C for five days under low oxygen conditions. After five days, the product was milled into a fine powder and heated again at 450 °C for another day, followed by dispersion in the aqueous solution of ferric chloride and hydrochloric acid. The final product is used as the precursor material for synthesizing graphene oxide by the Hummers' method (Akhavan et al., 2014). Adolfsson et al. synthesized graphene oxide quantum dots (QDs) using waste papers in another study. Cellulose present in the papers was collected and hydrothermally degraded at 160 °C in the presence of sulfuric acid as a catalyst, resulting in the formation of carbon nanospheres. The prepared carbon nanospheres were further converted into graphene oxide QDs by a heated sonication process in a 70% nitric acid solution (Adolfsson et al., 2015). Fig. 7 shows the SEM image of (a) cellulose and (b) carbon nanosphere. Ratan et al. synthesized activated carbon from agricultural waste using simple pyrolysis involving chemical activation (Ratan et al., 2018). These studies concluded that various kinds of waste materials could be transformed into useful products of carbonaceous material and used in various applications. The next part of the review will discuss the advantages of waste-derived methods over conventional methods for synthesizing carbon nanomaterial.

Metals such as iron, cobalt, nickel, platinum, gold, silver, titanium, and others have been employed as dopants in CNs. The metals will be chosen based on the CNs' desired characteristics for specific applications. Usually, doping of metals within the CNs improves various properties such as catalytic activity, electrical conductivity, thermal stability, mechanical stability, photocatalytic activity, antibacterial activity, and biocompatibility, which makes it suitable for numerous applications, including energy storage, sensor development, catalytic activities, environmental remediation, agriculture, and biomedical applications (Bai et al., 2021; Bayatsarmadi et al., 2017; Jiang et al., 2023; Li et al., 2022; Omran and Baek, 2022; Tejwan et al., 2021). In the metal-doping process, the metal nanoparticles/ions are incorporated into the carbon matrix, providing CNs with unique chemical and physical properties (Venna et al., 2022). Based on their properties and application, some of the impacts of the metal-doped CNs are categorized. (1) Catalytic property, the incorporation of the metals within the WD-CNs significantly changes their structure, subsequently changing their electronic properties directly related to catalytic activity. For instance, Kumar et al. have used metal-doped activated carbon fibre for catalytic degradation of diclofenac (Kumar et al., 2020). (2) Energy storage: some of the CNs, such as graphene oxide, CNTs, CNFs, and carbon beads, have shown high energy storage capacity after doping of metals. Therefore, metal doping makes the WD-CNs more suitable for application in batteries and supercapacitors (Huang et al., 2022). (3) Environmental remediation: Metal doping in WD-CNs makes them more precise for a particular pollutant. Therefore, metal-doped CNs efficiently remove pollutants such as dyes, heavy metals, and toxic chemicals from wastewater (Sultana et al., 2022). (4) Biocompatibility and antimicrobial property, metal doping in the WD-CNs makes them more stable and reduces their toxicity of metals. Additionally, some of the metal-NPs provide antimicrobial properties to the CNs. Therefore, it is used in wound healing material and cancer therapy (Ashfaq et al., 2013; Bhadauriya et al., 2018). The literature suggested that the metal-doped WD-CNs have exclusive impacts on environmental remediation and energy storage applications. Additionally, provides new opportunities for their use in biochemical and biomedical applications. The physical and chemical characteristics of WD-CNs can be considerably influenced by the doping of metals. With the help of metal-doping, we can easily tuned the electrical conductivity, catalytic activity, magnetic characteristics, structural stability, and optical properties. The metal-doped-CNs are a versatile and favourable class of materials for various applications because these effects can be altered to suit specific applications. However, more studies are required to understand the mechanistic way of analyzing metal-doped-CNs and their applicability.

The WD-CNs have been used in various applications, including catalysis, energy storage, removal of heavy metals and other pollutants from wastewater, sensors, and biochemical and biomedical applications (Buruga et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2022a). The catalytic properties of NMs are efficient for environmental remediation applications. Synthesis of NMs from rice husk or other biomass makes a direction for agricultural waste management (Sarkar et al., 2018). Additionally, WD-CNs were found to be suitable candidates for fluorescent materials that do not contain harmful materials. Metal-doped WD-CNs, such as carbon dots and CNTs, are widely used in biomedical and fluorescent-based sensors (Yu et al., 2022).

Energy storage is a major need for all technological applications, including electronic products. The production and storage of energy are essential processes for energy utilization. Numerous methods and processes, including catalysis, water splitting, photocatalysis, and microbial fuel cells (MFCs), are used to produce energy. Moreover, supercapacitors are energy storage devices with several properties, including efficient power density, long life cycle, and quick charge and discharge potential (Rawat et al., 2022). Usually, rechargeable batteries have high energy density and low discharge rates compared with disposable batteries (Mankge et al., 2022; Senthil and Lee, 2021). Researchers continue looking towards newer supercapacitor materials with high energy storage ability. In this aspect, it is necessary to develop newer supercapacitor materials that overcome such issues in existing materials.

Usually, electrode materials are one of the important factors affecting the battery's efficiency. Significant efforts have been made to develop materials and their uses in supercapacitors. WD-CNs provide newer wings to supercapacitors due to their intrinsic characteristics like high surface area, porosity, ease of functionalization, and mechanical strength. Numerous metal-doped-WD-CNs have been used to develop efficient electrode materials for energy storage applications. For instance, Wong et al. used silicon husk to produce silicon-doped graphene as an anode material for lithium-ion batteries. The material exhibits high current density (⁓1000 mA h g−1) at a high initial capacity (⁓1000 mA h g−1) for up to 30 cycles (Wong et al., 2014). Similarly, Li et al. used virgin Si/graphene, which showed high cyclic stability and storage capacity. These materials are efficient in storage applications; however, Si NPs have low coulombic efficiency, limiting their life cycle as anode material due to rapid surface reactions and thermodynamic stability (Li et al., 2013). To overcome this problem, researchers have developed Si-doped CNTs with high electrical conductivity and robust structure. CNTs have crosslinked structures that efficiently transport electrons among the active particles (Praneetha and Murugan, 2015). Kanamori et al. synthesized the waste batteries' catalyst for hydrogen production through the methane dry reforming process. The waste batteries contain Ni-metal hybrid compounds, Ni, alongside La, Co, C, and Mn. The final product of these waste materials produces NiO-enriched carbon material. That NiO helps catalyze syngas generation (H2 and CO) from greenhouse gasses. The battery waste-derived NiO was also more active and stable than commercially available NiO (Kanamori et al., 2009). Xing et al. have pyrolyzed the sugar industry waste to synthesize CNs. The authors have also optimized the physical parameters of the pyrolysis process, such as gas flow, heating rate, and temperature, to change the morphology of the carbon product. The material was used as an anode in electrochemical applications and showed approximately 650 mAh g−1 reversible capacity (Xing et al., 1996). In a study, Gao et al. have synthesized biochar by pyrolysis of waste crab shells. The material was pyrolyzed at 900 OC temperature and showed a high surface area (⁓634 m2 g−1). The synthesized biochar was used in supercapacitor applications and showed high specific capacitance (220 F/g) and good rate ability (⁓97.93%) even after 5000 cycles (Gao et al., 2018). Similarly, many other waste materials, including fir wood, rubber wood sawdust, and coffee endocarp, have been transformed into WD-CNs and activated through a physical activation process to use as supercapacitor electrodes (Chithra et al., 2018; Gao et al., 2016; Ko et al., 2016; Mondal et al., 2017; Qu et al., 2016; Ru et al., 2016). The literature mentioned above, and the comparative data of Table 4 suggested that the WD-CNs are efficiently used for energy storage applications due to their exceptional characteristics like porous texture, pore-size size distribution, easy-to-tune surface texture, and functional group, which make them a promising candidate for energy storage devices. Moreover, metal-doped WD-CNs attract more research due to their cost effectiveness, efficient performance, and abundant availability.

Biomedical sectors utilize CNs for various applications, including wound healing, drug delivery, antimicrobial therapies, and bioimaging. Some carbonaceous materials, such as carbon dots, CNFs, CNTs, and metal-doped CNs, have gained major attention due to their unique properties, including antibacterial nature, strong luminescence, biocompatibility, and amenability to surface modifications. The carbonaceous materials were synthesized using conventional methods such as carbonization of polymers, pyrolysis of saccharides, laser ablation of graphite, etc., and are cost-efficient (Samaddar et al., 2018). Therefore, researchers have used waste materials to synthesize CNs for economic and cost efficiency. Doping of Metal NPs in CNs provides them with unique properties like antibacterial nature, thermal and physical stability, and activity efficiency (Omar et al., 2022), making them more efficient for applications in the biological or biomedical area. Park et al. synthesized carbon dots from food waste (FW) and used them to bioimage human hepatocellular liver carcinoma (HepG2) cells in a study. Approximately 100 kg of FW produced 1.2 g of 2–4 nm carbon dots (Park et al., 2014). In another study, Moradi et al. used wood waste to synthesize carbon dots in bioimaging applications. Other than bioimaging, WD-CNs have also been used in Alzheimer's disease curation (Moradi et al., 2019). Fan et al. have synthesized curcumin-loaded transferrin-mediated liposomes and used them with the α-mangosteen, a potential candidate for treating Alzheimer's disease (Fan et al., 2018). The metal-doped WD CNs have also been used in various disease diagnoses and treatments, including tumour diagnosis, hyperthermia, gastroenterological diseases, and tuberculosis (Alqahtani et al., 2017; Hagl et al., 2015; Iacob et al., 2021; Nagraik et al., 2021). Apart from this, these materials have also been used in drug delivery applications due to their efficient encapsulation and target-mediated distribution (Varma, 2019). Some metal nanoparticles, such as iron oxide NPs and gold nanorods, provide magnetic and plasmonic characteristics to CNs, making them efficient for target-mediated drug delivery applications (Ali and Ahmed, 2018). Athinarayanan et al. synthesized carbon nanodots from waste fish scales in a study. The carbon dots exhibit fluorescent characteristics and are efficiently used as a probe for fluorescence probe for cellular imaging (Athinarayanan et al., 2020). Similarly, Thakur et al. used waste citrus limetta pulp to synthesize CDs. These CDs exhibit antibacterial properties along with bioimaging, water splitting, and methylene blue degradation applications (Thakur et al., 2019). Similarly, many other studies have been done on the biomedical applications of WD-CNs (Malode et al., 2023; Veeramani et al., 2015), some of which are briefly depicted in Table 4. The studies mentioned above and relative data suggested that WD-CNs were effectively used for biomedical applications that revolutionized the next-generation biomedical devices and biomaterials using WD-CNs.

Apart from the above application, other studies have also been done on utilizing waste-derived carbonaceous materials. For example, Jahan et al. have synthesized nanohydroxyapatite using eggshell-derived calcium precursor through a reverse microemulsion process at optimum temperature. Approximately 5–20 nm-sized NPs of nanohydroxyapatite were formed using this process. Further, the material was calcined at 600 OC temperature and used in arsenic (As(V)) removal application. The experimental data confirm that the synthesized material can efficiently remove the As(V) from the wastewater (Akter Jahan et al., 2017). Similarly, Hu et al. synthesized fluorescent carbon NPs from waste plastic bags as a carbon source by degradation through a thermal-oxidation process followed by polymerization, carbonization process, and a passivation process. The NPs were functionalized with various oxygen moieties, exhibiting excellent optical stability, less cytotoxicity, outstanding photoluminescence quantum yield, and effective ability to disperse in water. The synthesized carbon NPs played a significant role as a fluorescent probe for detecting Fe(III) (Hu et al., 2014b). Similarly, activated WD-CNs for methylene blue adsorption (Liu et al., 2018), N-WD-CNs for the removal of chloramphenicol and sulfamethazine pharmaceutical compounds (Qin et al., 2016), adsorption of acid red 18 (Wang et al., 2018a), recovery of antibiotics (Mohd Din et al., 2015), and detection of lead and cadmium (Zeinu et al., 2016). The literature suggests that the WD-CNs might be next-generation materials for end applications due to their versatility and cost-effectiveness. We definitely have plenty of reasons to choose WD-CNs, mainly because they are cost-effective and provide newer avenues for the circular economy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of WD-CNs in a circular economy
ce:section.ce:para: CNs are created from a variety of waste materials, such as biomass, plastic, industrial, agricultural, etc. The fundamental benefit of synthesizing WD-CNs is that it turns waste materials into useful products or items with added value, and it lessens environmental pollution. The burning of waste in landfills is also stopped by WD-CNs, which reduces greenhouse gas emissions and other negative environmental effects [119, 161, 162].

Undeniably, the WD-CNs can considerably affect the development of a circular economy mainly by reducing waste, reusing resources, and regenerating natural systems. Transforming waste materials from different sources into valuable materials like CNs can be applied in numerous applications. Usually, there are mainly five main ways that affect the circular economy. (1) Reduced waste materials, WD-CNs might significantly reduce the waste by converting CNs that might be used into different valuable products, subsequently reducing the landfill space and their environmental impacts. (2) Newer resources, with the synthesis of WD-CNs industries, can significantly reduce their dependency on fossil fuels, as we can use waste materials as a source of Carbon. We can say that WD-CNs significantly reduce the demand for fossil fuels, natural resources and adverse environmental effects. (3) Saving energy, WD-CNs might be supported to save energy by significantly reducing the need for energy-intensive production. In other words, we can say that using waste materials as a resource reduces the energy required to extract and process fossil fuels. (4) Revenue generation: WD-CNs create new possibilities for generating revenue by transforming WMs into valuable products. Moreover, this transformation into a valuable product can decrease the cost of waste disposal and newer business approaches. (5) Reduce adverse effects on the environment by synthesizing WD-CNs industries significantly reduces the WMs, conserves natural resources, and decreases energy consumption that might decrease the emission of greenhouse gases and pollution (air and water) (Awasthi et al., 2022; Chin, 2023; Coste-Maniere et al., 2019; Duan et al., 2022; Kurniawan et al., 2023; Ma et al., 2023; Picón-Núñez, 2023; Velenturf and Purnell, 2021).

WD-CNs from various sources (like biomass, plastic waste, IW, agricultural waste and other waste) play an essential role in the circular economy. WD-CNs have unique characteristics according to their source that make them efficient in several applications, including environmental remediation, energy, adsorption, agriculture, and catalytic activity. WD-CNs applications can potentially provide multiple benefits in the circular economy, including waste reduction resources, conservation, and economic development. However, managing the potential risks associated with their synthesis and applications is necessary.

CNs-based materials have revolutionized numerous aspects of biomedicine by controlling bacterial/viral infection, inhibiting cancerous cells, disease diagnosis, and drug delivery. Moreover, it is important to understand the behaviour of any materials against different cell-lines or biological systems. Although biocompatible test assay is essential to understand the adverse effects of materials, their association between physio-chemical characteristics and specific cells/tissues is essential to broaden their applicability (Hu et al., 2017; Kyriakides et al., 2021; Syama and Mohanan, 2016). The biocompatibility of the materials or WD-CNs might depend on the sources, types of materials, and types of cells. Some of them are briefly discussed in this section.

Numerous types of WD-CNs have been developed from OW, including carbon-QDs (C-QDs), graphene, CNTs, biochar, hydrochar, etc. and used in different applications, including biological applications like bioimaging, diagnosis, and treatment of disease (Umar et al., 2023). For instance, Zhao et al. synthesized WD-C-QD from Mussels using hydrothermal processes for bioimaging applications against zebra fish (embryos and larvae). The data suggested that the C-QD effectively analyzed embryos and larvae under fluorescence microscopy. The fluorescent intensity clearly indicates that the C-QD have the potential ability as fluorescent probes. Fig. 8 shows the bioimaging of onion epidermal cells and human liver cancer cell lines (HepG2). The data indicate that the HepG2 cells show 100% viability after incubation for 24 h. On the other hand, Onion cells show fluorescence similar to that of control cells, which indicates the C-QD does not cross the cytoplasmic membrane (Zhao et al., 2022).

Similarly, a particular investigation by Chung et al. synthesized WD-CDs using sugar cane bagasse char by a hydrothermal process at different temperatures (150, 170, 190, and 210 °C). The data suggested that the 190 °C shows high fluorescent intensity, which was incorporated with hydroxyapatite (HAP) to produce HAP-CDs to deliver acetaminophen. The prepared HAP-CDs show exceptional biocompatibility and solubility with high loading ability. Therefore, the prepared HAP-CDs show exceptional drug delivery ability that significantly enhances therapeutic efficacy (Chung et al., 2020). Lakshmanan et al. synthesized C-QDs from orange peels using a hydrothermal process and used them as anti-microbial agents. The data suggested that the prepared C-QDs show exceptional anti-microbial ability with high biocompatibility (Lakshmanan et al., 2021). These studies suggested that the WD-CNs from OW have high biocompatibility, thereby effectively used for bioimaging, drug delivery, and diagnosis of diseases.

Like OW, plastic WD-CNs were also used in numerous biological applications. For instance, Liang et al. synthesized CDs from plastic waste using pyrolysis and hydrothermal processes for agricultural applications. The data suggested that the CDs significantly improved the germination rate and root-shoot growth and increased moisture level, chlorophyll, and carbohydrate content within the pea plants. Moreover, no phytotoxicity was observed up to 2 mg/mL dose attributed to high biocompatibility (Liang et al., 2023). Similarly, Chauhan et al. synthesized CDs using plastic waste (bottles, polybags, and cups) for agricultural applications. The data suggested that the CDs significantly increase the germination rate and chlorophyll content of all four types of seeds (Cicer arietinum, Vigna radiata, Triticum aestivum, and Hordeum vulgare. Additionally, CDs show the dose-dependent effects (250–5000 mg/L) on all types of seeds, including roots and coleoptile length. Moreover, seedlings show exceptional physiological stability at 3000 mg/L concentration, which indicates the high biocompatibility of CDs against plants (Chauhan et al., 2022). Mezni et al. synthesized CNTs from plastic waste using the CVD process. The prepared CNTs were loaded with doxorubicin (DOX) drugs and then encapsulated with chitosan (CH) to produce CNTs-DOX-CH based nano-capsules for cancer treatment. The data suggested that the prepared CNTs-DOX-CH based nano-capsules increase the death rate of cancer cells by approximately 500 folds. Moreover, the prepared CNTs-DOX–CH–based nano-capsules did not show any toxic response, thereby high biocompatibility (Mezni et al., 2017). Chaudhary et al. synthesized C-QDs from plastic waste using pyrolysis and carbonization processes for sensing applications. The data suggested that the prepared C-QDs show excellent biocompatibility (Chaudhary et al., 2021). These studies suggested that plastic WD-CNs also have high biocompatibility, thereby making them safer to use for biomedical applications.

Like OW and PW, studies suggested that the industrial WD-CNs also showed a biocompatible nature when they were used in a biological system. For instance, Alamdari et al. synthesized C-QDs from agro-industry waste by using a hydrothermal process. The synthesized C-QDs were used for antibacterial applications, whereas cellular toxicity was observed using a mouse fibroblast cell line. The data suggested that the non-toxic effect against the mouse fibroblast cell line at a dose of 400 μg/ml, which showed 81% cell viability after 24 h of incubation, which was similar to the control cell line, conforming to the non-toxic effect against the biological system and are biocompatible in nature (Alamdari et al., 2023). Pandiyan et al. synthesized biocompatible C-QDs from the waste of the sugarcane industry. The waste was converted to C-QDs by using a hydrothermal carbonization process. The data suggested that the C-QDs show fluorescent ability with high biocompatibility (Pandiyan et al., 2020). These data suggested that the WD-CNs are highly biocompatible in nature. However, we cannot observe the toxicity on the basis of the source of materials. Moreover, toxicity depends on the types of materials, dose-dependent, and types of cells. Therefore, we can say that WD-CNs might be used for numerous biological applications, including bioimaging, biosensors, drug delivery, plant growth promotion, and nano-carrier applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of WD-CNs on environmental issues and waste management
ce:section.ce:para: Usually, WD-CNs employ both positive and negative impacts on environmental issues and waste management. In view of the positive aspect, waste materials converted into products like CNs have profound implications across environmental, economic, and social dimensions. WD-CNs have unique characteristics that enable newer avenues in environmental remediation (water and air purification by adsorption/filtration process, photocatalytic degradation, and anti-microbial agents), catalyst, energy, agriculture, and biomedical applications. From an environmental perspective, waste-to-product conversion directly addresses the urgent issue of waste accumulation in landfills (Ma et al., 2017). By repurposing waste into valuable products or energy, this approach significantly reduces the burden on disposal sites, contributing to the mitigation of environmental degradation associated with conventional waste disposal. Furthermore, waste to CNs conversion promotes resource conservation by extracting valuable materials from waste streams. This lessens the demand for virgin raw materials, thus aiding in the preservation of ecosystems and biodiversity (Mubarik et al., 2021). The environmental impact extends to the reduction of greenhouse gas emissions, particularly through waste-to-energy technologies that generate electricity or heat by processing or incinerating waste (Qian et al., 2022). Economically, waste-to-product conversion facilitates resource recovery, leading to cost savings and the creation of new economic opportunities. The industry has the potential to generate employment in various sectors, including research and development, engineering, manufacturing, and maintenance (Malode et al., 2023). As technologies evolve, the demand for skilled and unskilled labour in these areas is likely to increase. Waste to CNs conversion also plays a role in the energy sector, contributing to the diversification of energy sources. Technologies such as anaerobic digestion can convert organic waste into biogas, offering a renewable and cleaner alternative to natural gas for heating and electricity generation (He et al., 2022). On a social level, WD-CNs positively influence community health by reducing the environmental impact of landfills on air and water quality. Additionally, these projects often involve community engagement, awareness campaigns, and education programs, fostering a sense of responsibility and environmental stewardship among the public (Chen et al., 2022b). Moreover, WD-CNs encourage innovation and research in technology and engineering (Manasa et al., 2022). Ongoing advancements in this field lead to the discovery of more efficient and sustainable methods for handling diverse types of waste, further contributing to the evolution of waste management practices. The process is a transformative approach that addresses the challenges of waste disposal while simultaneously offering environmental, economic, and social benefits. By redefining waste as a valuable resource, this process contributes to the development of a more sustainable and circular economy (Chen et al., 2022a; Hosseinzadeh-Bandbafha et al., 2022).

In general, CNs and their release into the environmental system are subjected to examination. Researchers still try to balance the CNs and their ethical considerations. Moreover, production cost, scalability, and waste management are the greatest challenges that need to be addressed. Ongoing research should be focused on the maximized positive impact of CNs that mitigate the possible drawbacks.

This review provides significant knowledge of the numerous ways to produce WD-CNs and their applications. WD-CNs have unique characteristics and are cost-effective, thereby easily used in various end applications. Furthermore, WD-CNs open new avenues for use in biochemical and biomedical applications. Moreover, the physicochemical properties of WD-CNs might be substantially influenced by the doping of metals. With the help of metal doping, we can easily tune the conductivity, catalytic activity, magnetic ability, structural stability, and optical properties. Therefore, we can say that the metal-doped WD-CNs are flexible and promising materials for end applications because these effects can be altered according to their respective applications. However, studies are required to understand the mechanistic way of analysing metal-doped-WD-CNs and their applicability. The circular economy may gain from WD-CN applications in various ways, including resource conservation, waste reduction, and economic growth. This review might give a newer insight into WD-CNs that significantly help researchers manage waste and transform waste materials into products, including CNs. Undeniably, this knowledge and understanding of WD-CNs might open a newer path for the young generation from waste to treasury.

Rishabh Anand Omar: Writing – original draft. Neetu Talreja: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Divya Chuhan: Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Mohammad Ashfaq: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
